content: 205. Statement of Ajay Kumar Chaudhary, brother of the deceased Vijay Chaudhary:

My brother Vijay had completed Grade 12 and worked as a contractor. He spontaneously joined the protest on Bhadra 24. He was behind the Parliament building when he was shot. Protesters took him to the hospital on a motorcycle, where he passed away. Security forces fired without warning. To prevent such incidents, the country must ensure employment, an end to corruption, and good governance. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief.
206. Statement of Keshav Budhathoki, father of the deceased Mahesh Budhathoki:

My eldest son Mahesh was a student who spontaneously joined the protest with friends. He last spoke with his mother. He was shot at Koteshwar Chowk and was already dead by the time protesters brought him to the hospital. The post-mortem confirms he died of a gunshot wound. Eyewitnesses say security forces fired aggressively without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission must ensure judicial action and push for policy reforms.
207. Statement of Sumitra Mahat, sister of the deceased Umesh Mahat:

My brother was an undergraduate student. He spontaneously participated in the movement organized by the Gen-Z generation against the then-government along with his friends. On Bhadra 23, Umesh participated in the Baneshwar protest, and on Bhadra 24, he was at Gaushala. The last time I spoke to him, he was at home. I don't know who took him to the hospital after he was shot while walking in Gaushala on Bhadra 24. Umesh was declared dead at Frontline Hospital that same day. Security personnel shot him in the chest in an aggressive and repressive manner. We have received NPR 1.5 million as relief from the Government of Nepal. The Commission must take judicial action against the guilty. Those responsible for the incident are all those at the policy level at that time, including the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, Chief District Officer, and Inspector General of Police.
208. Statement of Dharmaraj Ojha, Information Officer of Global College Kathmandu:

I have been working at Kathmandu Global College since its establishment. On Bhadra 23, 2082, after the first shift students were dismissed at 11:00 AM, some studentsShriyam Chaulagain and Samrat Jung Karkiconvinced the driver of the college-rented bus, Karna Bahadur Pulami, that they would get off early. They got off near Bharosa Hospital to personally participate in the demonstration. No one in management was aware of this. We only learned about the shooting in Baneshwar around 5:00 PM that evening. Shortly after, news of student casualties began appearing in the media. Upon learning that the situation outside had deteriorated, we handed over the afternoon shift students to their parents. We confirmed that a student from our college was among the casualties only after seeing the deceased's photo in the media. Seven days later, we visited Shriyam Chaulagain’s home and learned he had informed his sister in Germany and his mother at home about his intent to join the protest. No incident occurred at our college on the 23rd, though police informed us of rumors on social media regarding the harassment of female students at a Global College hostel. We informed the police that we do not operate a hostel and no such incident occurred. On Bhadra 24, protesters entered the college, causing massive damage through vandalism and arson. We have filed applications regarding the damages with both the police and this Commission. While the building, servers, computers, and smartboards are insured with Sanima GIC Insurance, the company is hesitating to provide the full reconstruction amount, which is hindering our ability to resume regular educational programs.
209. Statement of Tulsiram Bhandari, Program Director of Orient College, Vasundhara:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, after the first shift concluded, all students were sent home in regular transport. The second shift began at 11:00 AM as usual, but after hearing news of tension in the New Baneshwar area around 1:00 PM, we closed classes at approximately 12:30 PM and sent the children home after informing their parents. We later discovered that four students from our college participated in the Gen-Z protest in their college uniforms. There was no institutional participation from the college management. It is understood that the students involved were from the morning shift of Grade 12, and they participated from their homes after being absent from college that day. No injuries or human casualties occurred among our students, nor was there any physical damage to the college.
210. Statement of Dr. Toshima Karki:

On Bhadra 23, I was attending a parliamentary committee meeting. Around 10:30 AM, after learning the protest in Baneshwar had escalated, I left by car but was stopped before reaching the Babarmahal bridge. I insisted on proceeding and took a lift on a motorcycle. When the motorcycle could no longer move forward, I walked through the back alleys of Minbhawan to reach Civil Hospital. On my way, I saw police firing in the alleys while protesters pelted stones; both sides were in a clash. I tried to reason with both the young protesters and the security personnel, but no one was in a state to listen. I reached Civil Hospital around 12:00 PM as the injured were starting to arrive. I stayed outside the hospital facilitating treatment. Protesters would often return to the movement immediately after receiving basic treatment, even when I urged them not to. I repeatedly went outside to ask security forces to stop their aggression, but they did not listen. Tear gas fired by the police even reached inside the hospital. The situation was heartbreaking. I stayed at the hospital that night assisting all the health workers who were working tirelessly.

On the morning of the 24th, the Baneshwar area remained tense. By 10:00 AM, the injured were being brought in again. I eventually entered the Parliament building premises, where I found a man lying on the ground. After checking his pulse and finding none, I had him taken to the hospital. I pleaded with protesters to stop the vandalism. I then returned to Civil Hospital to assist with treatment until late at night, coordinating the transfer of critical patients to other hospitals. I stayed at the hospital that night as well and finally left on the morning of Bhadra 25. This event was extremely tragic; I did not see the police exercise any restraint.
211. Summary of Dil Bhusan Pathak’s statement to the Commission:

During the Gen-Z protests on Bhadra 23 and 24, major landmarks including the Federal Parliament, Singha Durbar, Supreme Court, and Hilton Hotel were vandalized or set on fire, and many lives were lost. When questioned if the arson at the Hilton Hotel was primarily caused by a video report aired on his YouTube channel 'Tough Talk with Dil Bhusan Pathak,' and asked about the fact-checking process and journalistic ethics followed, he stated: 'Journalism is the backbone of democracy. In today's society, professional journalism is not just a medium for information but a moral compass for the nation. Journalism cannot remain silent against bad governance and corruption. It is the primary responsibility of investigative journalism to expose the abuse of power, scams, and irregularities. The Constitution of Nepal ensures the freedom of press and expression, providing the legal basis to speak the truth. However, freedom comes with responsibility. Maintaining truth, impartiality, and balance is the core of professionalism. My belief is in the courage to speak the truth and stand with the people. Journalism makes the government accountable. For 33 years, I have been active through journalism for the rights and interests of the country and the people, and I am committed to remaining so until my last breath.'
212. Statement of Rajendra Kumar Sharma, Former President of NRNA:

It would be a great mistake to understand the Gen-Z movement of Bhadra 23 and 24 as merely a political event. This movement is a social explosion resulting from poverty, unemployment, rising economic inequality, and the state's weak service delivery. Approximately 8 million Nepalis living abroad are worried about the country's condition. The absolute zero accountability of the state is the root cause of this movement.
213. Statement from the Federation of Nepalese Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FNCCI):

The youth movement last Bhadra clearly demanded political change along with decent employment, economic prosperity, and socio-economic development. The younger generation expects entrepreneurship and job security through technology-based business development. To realize these expectations, the strength of the private sector is essential. However, the series of attacks and arson against private property, industries, and business establishments during the movement has severely demoralized the private sector. Development is impossible without private sector participation. Therefore, to rebuild confidence and prevent such incidents in the future, it is necessary for the Government of Nepal to approve and implement a 'Strategic Plan for the Protection and Promotion of the Private Sector,' drawing from the experiences of other countries that have faced similar unrest.
Detailed statements and incident reports are included in Annex 5.

grid.content: राष्ट्रीय सभा अध्यक्ष नारायण प्रसाद दाहाल

content: Chapter 6: Incident Details Received from Chief District Officers of Heavily Impacted Districts

To ascertain the situation before, during, and after the movement organized by the Gen-Z generation, the Commission requested incident reports from the then-Chief District Officers (CDOs) of the districts most affected by the protests. These reports received from the districts have been utilized as primary data for investigation and inquiry. Brief summaries of the incident reports and statements provided by the requested CDOs are presented below.
6.1. Koshi Province
6.1.1. District Administration Office, Morang

I served as the CDO at the District Administration Office, Morang, from January 07, 2025, to October 31, 2025. Subsequently, since November 03, 2025, I have been serving as the CDO of Mahottari district. Regarding the events of September 07, 2026 (Bhadra 24) involving the Gen-Z generationwho demanded national interest, good governance, and corruption controlit appears that attacks on public and private property continued until the following morning due to personal vendettas, even after the deployment of the Nepal Army on the night of the 24th. Per your request for a factual report on the major incidents, their causes, the key individuals or groups involved, and the status of security coordination in Morang, I provide the following details:
Status of Bhadra 23 (September 06):

Despite continuous heavy rainfall from 6:00 AM to 12:30 PM, representatives arrived at the office at 11:00 AM to request permission for a peaceful program. They specified a gathering of 700–1,000 people at Himalayan Road, Biratnagar, to advocate for good governance and the lifting of social media bans. Approximately 800 people participated. Although minor demonstrations targeted the Land Revenue Office and the Metropolitan Office in the afternoon, the situation remained otherwise normal. No force was used, and no injuries occurred. However, protesters became agitated following news of fatalities in Itahari (2 deaths) and Kathmandu. Coordination meetings were held with PABSON/N-PABSON to close local schools for three days. A Provincial Security Coordination Committee meeting was held at 6:00 PM for briefing; no untoward incidents occurred in Morang that day.
Status of Bhadra 24 (September 07):

A command post meeting was held at the District Administration Office at 6:00 AM to discuss preventing loss of life and property. Following verbal coordination with the Ministry of Home Affairs and the Provincial Ministry of Internal Affairs, a curfew was issued starting at 11:00 AM. Every effort was made to protect the Provincial Assembly buildinga primary targetand it remained secure until 2:00 PM. However, at that time, the Internal Revenue Office building at Traffic Chowk was set on fire, and the situation began to slip out of control. When social media and news reports claimed the Prime Minister had resigned and Singha Durbar was on fire, the morale of deployed security personnel weakened as they perceived a nationwide collapse of order. Arson then began at the Provincial Assembly building, followed by Koirala Residence, and eventually, protesters breached the District Administration Office around 3:00 PM. Attacks then spread to other government offices, private properties, and party offices. Prior to these events, a 10:00 AM meeting with local political parties had issued a public appeal for peace, and student unions were consulted to maintain order. The District Security Committee remained together on the ground until 7:00 PM, by which time 155 government offices, private establishments, homes, and political offices had sustained damage.
Causes of the Incident:
The primary cause was deep-seated anger toward the state and a desire for revenge following the deaths in Kathmandu and Itahari.
Main Individuals/Groups Involved:

The movement was fundamentally led by Gen-Z protesters. However, it was socially rumored afterward that certain 'social activists,' anti-establishment factions within political parties, and individuals posing as journalists on social media incited the crowd. I did not personally recognize these individuals.
Security Deployment and Coordination:

Because no force was used on Bhadra 23, our security analysis did not anticipate the following day's escalation. There was no lack of coordination among security agencies. After the Nepal Army was deployed at 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24, there were attempts to set fire to the Nepal Oil Corporation’s regional fuel depot and facilitate a jailbreak at the prison, but our local coordination and security deployment successfully prevented those outcomes.

Overall, while there were no physical injuries or dismemberment due to the use of force in this district, we were unable to protect the district from arson once reports of nationwide fires began to spread. No further damage to public or private property occurred after the Army took control. This statement was provided by the then-CDO of Morang, Indra Dev Yadav (Annex 6).
6.1.2. District Administration Office, Makwanpur

Per orders, I provide the following details regarding the events of September 07, 2026 (Bhadra 24) involving Gen-Z youth, including the attacks on property that occurred into the following morning and the coordination of security forces in Makwanpur district.
Major Timeline of Events: September 06, 2026 (Bhadra 23):

At 10:00 AM, approximately 1,200–1,300 youths and students from 'Gen-Z Youth Against Corruption' gathered in front of the Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City office in Ward No. 2. They protested against corruption and social media restrictions. The rally moved from the municipal office through Piple, Buddha Chowk, Sima Chowk, and TN Road to the Provincial Assembly office. After chanting slogans, they returned via Ram Mandir, Bypass Road, and Sima Chowk to Buddha Chowk. The group dispersed peacefully at 3:15 PM. A joint team of the Armed Police Force and Nepal Police was deployed at the site.
Major Timeline of Events: September 07, 2082 (Bhadra 24):

content.content: Around 09:00 AM: Approximately 100–200 youths associated with 'Gen-Z Youth Against Corruption' gathered at Sima Chowk, Hetauda-4. They protested against corruption and the ban on social media. The crowd grew to 1,500–2,000, advancing while vandalizing and committing arson against government offices.
10:45 AM: Gen-Z supporters (100–200) vandalized student union banners inside Makwanpur Multipurpose Campus near Manakamana Chowk, Hetauda-2.
11:30 AM: Protesters vandalized the Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City signboard at Yuddha Chowk.
11:50 AM: Around 200–250 Gen-Z supporters in Manahari-7 broke the locks of Nepali Congress, CPN-UML, and CPN-Maoist party offices near the Area Police Office, burning furniture in the street.
12:10 PM: Protesters set fire to the CPN-UML party office at Pushpalal Park, Hetauda-4.

12:45 PM: The three-story District Coordination Committee building in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by arson. Two motorcycles and a Hilux vehicle (Ba 1 Jha 9430) at the Industry and Commerce Office were burned.
12:54 PM: The District Land Revenue Office in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by arson and vandalism.

01:30 PM: A six-story building in Hetauda-1 housing several provincial ministries (Agriculture, Infrastructure, Labor & Transport, and Water & Energy) was completely destroyed. Arson consumed 2 Scorpios, 1 car, 12 motorcycles, and 4 scooters in the parking lot.
01:40 PM: Protesters vandalized the Bagmati Provincial Ministry of Economic Affairs and Planning, burning furniture and government motorcycles.
01:55 PM: The Commission for the Investigation of Abuse of Authority (CIAA) office in Hetauda-1 was vandalized, and vehicles were set on fire.
02:00 PM: The Inland Revenue Office in Hetauda-2 was attacked. A canteen and a two-story office building were completely burned, along with 6 motorcycles and 2 cars.

02:10 PM: The Hetauda Bench of the Patan High Court was set on fire. The three-story building, furniture, and windows were destroyed. Five motorcycles and 2 taxis were burned, and 4 cars were vandalized.

02:20 PM: The High Government Attorney’s Office and District Government Attorney’s Office were attacked. Arson destroyed the first floor of the District office and the first floor of the High office, with subsequent floors vandalized. Two cars, 6 motorcycles, and 4 bicycles were incinerated.

02:35 PM: Protesters breached the Bagmati Province Assembly building, causing total destruction by fire. Two parliamentary party buildings, a canteen, and a library were destroyed. A Rastriya Banijya Bank ATM was vandalized, and 6 vehicles were burned.

02:45 PM: The four-story building housing the Ministry of Health and Ministry of Social Development was entirely destroyed. A warehouse and canteen were burned, along with 25 cars, 15 motorcycles, and 7 scooters.
02:55 PM: The Manahari Rural Municipality office (Ward No. 9) was set on fire.
03:45 PM: Protesters entered and set fire to the Area Police Office in Newarpani, Hetauda-19.
03:55 PM: A 10-room yard belonging to Baniya Construction Service in Tintapke was destroyed. Vandalism and arson affected 19 tippers, 3 cement bulkers, and a JCB.
04:10 PM: The digital board and CCTV cameras at the Ward No. 1 office in China Quarter were vandalized.
04:20 PM: One Scorpio, one pickup, and two motorcycles were burned at the Vehicle Examination Office in Thanabharyang.
04:25 PM: The Transport Management Office in Thanabharyang was completely destroyed by arson, including 3 vehicles in the parking lot.
04:30 PM: Narayani Transport Business Company Pvt. Ltd. was vandalized and set on fire.

04:35 PM: The Basamadi Police Post was completely destroyed. Personal belongings and food supplies of 8 police personnel were incinerated. Nineteen motorcycles, one scooter, and one ambulance were burned.
04:40 PM: The two-story Urban Development and Building Office in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by fire.

05:00 PM: Protesters breached the Makwanpur District Court. Arson destroyed the six-story building and several vehicles (1 Sumo, 1 Bolero, 1 pickup, 46 motorcycles, and 7 bicycles). The canteen was also vandalized.
05:05 PM: The Treasury and Accounts Controller Office was attacked. Windows and doors were damaged, computers broken, and files scattered. A generator, one Sumo, and three motorcycles were burned.
05:10 PM: The residence of the Bagmati Province Chief Secretary was completely destroyed by arson.
05:20 PM: The Ministry of Internal Affairs and Law was vandalized. Protesters stole a TV, a laptop, and NPR 30,000 cash. Seven vehicles were burned.
05:30 PM: The three-story Survey (Napi) Office was completely destroyed by arson, including three vehicles in the parking lot.
05:40 PM: The Public Service Commission (Lok Sewa Aayog) office was vandalized, and two vehicles were burned.
05:55 PM: The Ministry of Industry, Commerce, Land, and Administration was vandalized, and three vehicles were set on fire.
06:10 PM: Arson was committed at the Ward No. 4 office and the District Sports Development Committee office.
06:15 PM: Protesters attacked the house of Bishwakanti Kalawati Sumargi (Ajay Sumargi), burning three cars and destroying 13 rooms, including furniture and electronics.
06:20 PM: The Agriculture building of Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City in Thanabharyang was pelted with stones, damaging all doors and windows of the three-story structure.
06:35 PM: Arson and vandalism were committed at the Provincial Emergency Operation Center (PEOC) and the District Traffic Police Office, Makwanpur.
06:35 PM: The Ministry of Cooperatives and Poverty Alleviation was attacked; the first floor was destroyed by fire while the second floor was vandalized.
06:40 PM: The Bagmati Province Police Disaster Management Gulm was attacked. Barracks, windows, and the mess area were damaged; furniture and a TV were broken, and 4 motorcycles were burned.
06:45 PM: A three-story ward office building in Hetauda-11 was vandalized and set on fire.
06:50 PM: The Madan Bhandari Academy of Health Sciences was attacked. A 12-room building was destroyed, along with 5 high-end vehicles (Prado, Scorpio, etc.).
07:00 PM: The seven-story Bhatbhateni Supermarket in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by vandalism, looting, and arson.
07:20 PM: The residence of Ananta Prasad Paudel (CPN-UML leader/former CA member) was attacked. Protesters stole jewelry and a projector; the house sustained partial arson damage.
07:25 PM: The two-story house of Hidam Lama (Chair of Bhimphedi Rural Municipality) was completely destroyed by arson.
07:30 PM: Three buildings of the Provident Fund (Sanchay Kosh) office were attacked, and 5 vehicles were incinerated.
07:35 PM: The residence of former Chief Minister Dor Mani Paudel was vandalized, causing external damage.
07:40 PM: The three-story residence of Chief Minister Indra Bahadur Baniya was completely destroyed by arson.

content: Major Timeline of Events: September 08, 2082 (Bhadra 25):

content.content: Around 12:55 PM: Inmates in Block 'A' of Bhimphedi Prison broke through three iron gates, launched a protest with stone-pelting, and breached the security perimeter. They converged at the main gate and committed arson/vandalism against the administration building, main gate, and APF barracks. Security forces (APF) fired 2 rounds from SLR and 6 tear gas shells. The Nepal Army (Kali Prasad Battalion) fired approximately 200 aerial rounds. Out of 554 inmates, 259 escaped via the western wall. Two inmates, Deepak Sunar and Naresh Murau, sustained gunshot wounds to their legs and were treated at Hetauda Hospital.

10:15 AM: While inmates in Block 'B' of Bhimphedi Prison were attempting a jailbreak by breaking the wall, APF forces used 5 tear gas shells to regain control. They detained 296 inmates, but 181 successfully escaped.

content: Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

content.content: Around 12:55 PM, inmates in Block 'A' of Bhimphedi Prison, Makwanpur, broke through three iron gates, chanting slogans and pelting stones. They breached the security perimeter, gathered at the main gate, and vandalized/arsoned the prison administration building, the main gate, the APF Prison Security Base barracks, the check post, and the administration area. In response, the APF team fired 2 rounds from SLR and 6 tear gas shells. A 70-member team from the Nepal Army (Kali Prasad Battalion) fired approximately 200 aerial rounds. Out of 554 inmates in that block, 259 escaped by demolishing the western wall. The remaining 295 were brought under control and returned to their blocks by 3:30 PM. Two inmates, 25-year-old Deepak Sunar (theft case) and 33-year-old Naresh Murau (homicide case), sustained leg injuries from gunfire and were treated at Hetauda Hospital before being returned to custody.

On September 08, 2082 (Bhadra 25) at 10:15 AM, while inmates in Block 'B' of Bhimphedi Prison were attempting to escape by breaking the wall, the APF Prison Security Base team fired 5 tear gas shells to control the situation. They successfully detained 296 inmates, while 181 managed to flee.

content: Regarding the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, all heads of district security agencies met in the Chief District Officer's (CDO) office to analyze the situation. Initially, force was not used. Deployment and coordination were carried out according to the Security Committee's decisions, as detailed in the incident report by the then CDO of Makwanpur, Skim Shrestha (Annex 6).
6.1.3. District Administration Office, Sunsari:

On Bhadra 23 at approximately 10:40 AM, about 1,000 students and youths led by Gen-Z campaign coordinator Srishti Bhattarai and local leader Bikal Regmi gathered at Itahari Golchowk and began a rally. The crowd eventually swelled to an estimated 4,000–4,500 people. During the rally, infiltrators began shouting provocative anti-government slogans, and the protest went beyond the leaders' control. Protesters vandalized the digital notice board at the Itahari Sub-Metropolitan City gate and tried to break the main gate. When security forces intervened, they were attacked with stones, petrol bombs, and slingshots. Some protesters entered the office, damaging two fire engines, a water tanker, and other vehicles, while setting fire to the Mayor's office and several administrative branches. The administration had previously requested Gen-Z representatives to obtain formal permission and warned them to remain vigilant against potential infiltration.
